question:
A-2. Consider the locus given by the real polynomial equation
\[
A x^{2}+B x y+C y^{2}+D x^{3}+E x^{2} y+F x y^{2}+G y^{3}=0
\]
where \( B^{\mathbf{s}}-4 A C<0 \). Prove that there is a positive number \( \delta \) such that there are no points of the locus in the punctured disk
\[
0<x^{2}+y^{2}<\delta^{2} .
\]
solution:
A-2 Let \( (x, y)=(r \cos \theta, r \sin \theta), r>0 \), be a point of the locus. Then
\[
r=\frac{\left|A \cos ^{2} \theta+B \sin \theta \cos \theta+C \sin ^{2} \theta\right|}{\left|D \cos ^{3} \theta+E \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta+F \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta+G \sin ^{3} \theta\right|}
\]

The denominator of (1) is less than or equal to \( |D|+|E|+|F|+|G| \), whereas the numerator has a positive minimum
\[
N=\frac{|A+C|-\sqrt{(A-C)^{2}+B^{2}}}{2}
\]
since \( B^{2}<4 A C \). Therefore
\[
r \geqq \frac{N}{|D|+|E|+|F|+|G|}=\delta
\]
and there are no points of the locus within \( 0<r<\delta \).
Alternate Solution: Set \( H(x, y) \) equal to the polynomial on the left hand side of the given equation. The standard theory for maxima or minima of functions of two variables can be used together with the condition \( B^{2}<4 A C \) to show that \( H(x, y) \) has a local maximum or a local minimum at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long.question:
A-2. Consider the locus given by the real polynomial equation
\[
firstcof xcoord^{2}+secondco xcoord ycoord+thirdcof ycoord^{2}+fourthco xcoord^{3}+fifthcoe xcoord^{2} ycoord+sixthcoe xcoord ycoord^{2}+seventhc ycoord^{3}=0
\]
where \( secondco^{\mathbf{s}}-4 firstcof thirdcof<0 \). Prove that there is a positive number \( deltaval \) such that there are no points of the locus in the punctured disk
\[
0<xcoord^{2}+ycoord^{2}<deltaval^{2} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2 Let \( (xcoord, ycoord)=(radius \cos anglevar, radius \sin anglevar), radius>0 \), be a point of the locus. Then
\[
radius=\frac{\left|firstcof \cos ^{2} anglevar+secondco \sin anglevar \cos anglevar+thirdcof \sin ^{2} anglevar\right|}{\left|fourthco \cos ^{3} anglevar+fifthcoe \cos ^{2} anglevar \sin anglevar+sixthcoe \cos anglevar \sin ^{2} anglevar+seventhc \sin ^{3} anglevar\right|}
\]

The denominator of (1) is less than or equal to \( |fourthco|+|fifthcoe|+|sixthcoe|+|seventhc| \), whereas the numerator has a positive minimum
\[
numerlow=\frac{|firstcof+thirdcof|-\sqrt{(firstcof-thirdcof)^{2}+secondco^{2}}}{2}
\]
since \( secondco^{2}<4 firstcof thirdcof \). Therefore
\[
radius \geqq \frac{numerlow}{|fourthco|+|fifthcoe|+|sixthcoe|+|seventhc|}=deltaval
\]
and there are no points of the locus within \( 0<radius<deltaval \).
Alternate Solution: Set \( polyfunc(xcoord, ycoord) \) equal to the polynomial on the left hand side of the given equation. The standard theory for maxima or minima of functions of two variables can be used together with the condition \( secondco^{2}<4 firstcof thirdcof \) to show that \( polyfunc(xcoord, ycoord) \) has a local maximum or a local minimum at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-2. Consider the locus given by the real polynomial equation
\[
compassrose shoreline^{2}+shipanchor shoreline driftwood+windcurrent driftwood^{2}+tidalcrest shoreline^{3}+coralridge shoreline^{2} driftwood+seagullcry shoreline driftwood^{2}+lighthouse driftwood^{3}=0
\]
where \( shipanchor^{\mathbf{s}}-4 compassrose windcurrent<0 \). Prove that there is a positive number \( mistyharbor \) such that there are no points of the locus in the punctured disk
\[
0<shoreline^{2}+driftwood^{2}<mistyharbor^{2} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-2 Let \( (shoreline, driftwood)=(sandgrain \cos sunhorizon, sandgrain \sin sunhorizon), sandgrain>0 \), be a point of the locus. Then
\[
sandgrain=\frac{\left|compassrose \cos ^{2} sunhorizon+shipanchor \sin sunhorizon \cos sunhorizon+windcurrent \sin ^{2} sunhorizon\right|}{\left|tidalcrest \cos ^{3} sunhorizon+coralridge \cos ^{2} sunhorizon \sin sunhorizon+seagullcry \cos sunhorizon \sin ^{2} sunhorizon+lighthouse \sin ^{3} sunhorizon\right|}
\]

The denominator of (1) is less than or equal to \( |tidalcrest|+|coralridge|+|seagullcry|+|lighthouse| \), whereas the numerator has a positive minimum
\[
barnacle=\frac{|compassrose+windcurrent|-\sqrt{(compassrose-windcurrent)^{2}+shipanchor^{2}}}{2}
\]
since \( shipanchor^{2}<4 compassrose windcurrent \). Therefore
\[
sandgrain \geqq \frac{barnacle}{|tidalcrest|+|coralridge|+|seagullcry|+|lighthouse|}=mistyharbor
\]
and there are no points of the locus within \( 0<sandgrain<mistyharbor \).
Alternate Solution: Set \( horizonline(shoreline, driftwood) \) equal to the polynomial on the left hand side of the given equation. The standard theory for maxima or minima of functions of two variables can be used together with the condition \( shipanchor^{2}<4 compassrose windcurrent \) to show that \( horizonline(shoreline, driftwood) \) has a local maximum or a local minimum at \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-2. Consider the locus given by the real polynomial equation
\[
variableone\ verticalaxis^{2}+variabletwo\ verticalaxis\ horizontalaxis+variablethree\ horizontalaxis^{2}+variablefour\ verticalaxis^{3}+variablefive\ verticalaxis^{2}\ horizontalaxis+variablesix\ verticalaxis\ horizontalaxis^{2}+variableseven\ horizontalaxis^{3}=0
\]
where \( variabletwo^{\mathbf{s}}-4\ variableone\ variablethree<0 \). Prove that there is a positive number \( infinite \) such that there are no points of the locus in the punctured disk
\[
0<verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}<infinite^{2} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2 Let \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis)=(diameter \cos directionless, diameter \sin directionless), diameter>0 \), be a point of the locus. Then
\[
diameter=\frac{\left|variableone \cos ^{2} directionless+variabletwo \sin directionless \cos directionless+variablethree \sin ^{2} directionless\right|}{\left|variablefour \cos ^{3} directionless+variablefive \cos ^{2} directionless \sin directionless+variablesix \cos directionless \sin ^{2} directionless+variableseven \sin ^{3} directionless\right|}
\]
The denominator of (1) is less than or equal to \( |variablefour|+|variablefive|+|variablesix|+|variableseven| \), whereas the numerator has a positive minimum
\[
maximumvalue=\frac{|variableone+variablethree|-\sqrt{(variableone-variablethree)^{2}+variabletwo^{2}}}{2}
\]
since \( variabletwo^{2}<4 variableone variablethree \). Therefore
\[
diameter \geqq \frac{maximumvalue}{|variablefour|+|variablefive|+|variablesix|+|variableseven|}=infinite
\]
and there are no points of the locus within \( 0<diameter<infinite \).

Alternate Solution: Set \( linearfunc(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) equal to the polynomial on the left hand side of the given equation. The standard theory for maxima or minima of functions of two variables can be used together with the condition \( variabletwo^{2}<4 variableone variablethree \) to show that \( linearfunc(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \) has a local maximum or a local minimum at \( (0,0) \).

garbled_string.question:
A-2. Consider the locus given by the real polynomial equation
\[
qprlsxwt qzxwvtnp^{2}+fhdmzaoilu qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+lqkstmve hjgrksla^{2}+rzkfgnow qzxwvtnp^{3}+ghsalnuv qzxwvtnp^{2} hjgrksla+wbxqzpeo qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{2}+tmdirfla hjgrksla^{3}=0
\]
where \( fhdmzaoilu^{\mathbf{s}}-4 qprlsxwt lqkstmve<0 \). Prove that there is a positive number \( alwopsnf \) such that there are no points of the locus in the punctured disk
\[
0<qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}<alwopsnf^{2} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-2 Let \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla)=(bdjfkepl \cos mvncxero, bdjfkepl \sin mvncxero), bdjfkepl>0 \), be a point of the locus. Then
\[
bdjfkepl=\frac{\left|qprlsxwt \cos ^{2} mvncxero+fhdmzaoilu \sin mvncxero \cos mvncxero+lqkstmve \sin ^{2} mvncxero\right|}{\left|rzkfgnow \cos ^{3} mvncxero+ghsalnuv \cos ^{2} mvncxero \sin mvncxero+wbxqzpeo \cos mvncxero \sin ^{2} mvncxero+tmdirfla \sin ^{3} mvncxero\right|}
\]

The denominator of (1) is less than or equal to \( |rzkfgnow|+|ghsalnuv|+|wbxqzpeo|+|tmdirfla| \), whereas the numerator has a positive minimum
\[
vczopral=\frac{|qprlsxwt+lqkstmve|-\sqrt{(qprlsxwt-lqkstmve)^{2}+fhdmzaoilu^{2}}}{2}
\]
since \( fhdmzaoilu^{2}<4 qprlsxwt lqkstmve \). Therefore
\[
bdjfkepl \geqq \frac{vczopral}{|rzkfgnow|+|ghsalnuv|+|wbxqzpeo|+|tmdirfla|}=alwopsnf
\]
and there are no points of the locus within \( 0<bdjfkepl<alwopsnf \).
Alternate Solution: Set \( ygrkdslm(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) equal to the polynomial on the left hand side of the given equation. The standard theory for maxima or minima of functions of two variables can be used together with the condition \( fhdmzaoilu^{2}<4 qprlsxwt lqkstmve \) to show that \( ygrkdslm(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \) has a local maximum or a local minimum at \( (0,0) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer n \geq  3.  
Let A be an n \times  n real symmetric positive-definite matrix and denote its smallest
eigen-value by   

  \lambda  := \lambda _min(A) > 0.

Let   

  C(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)= \sum _{|\alpha |=3} c_\alpha  x^\alpha  (homogeneous of degree 3),  
  D(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)= \sum _{|\beta |=4} d_\beta  x^\beta  (homogeneous of degree 4),

where \alpha =(\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _n), |\alpha |=\alpha _1+\cdots +\alpha _n, x^\alpha =x_1^{\alpha _1}\cdots x_n^{\alpha _n}, and analogously for \beta .  
Put  

  M_3 := max_{|\alpha |=3}|c_\alpha |  (if C\equiv 0 set M_3:=0),  
  M_4 := max_{|\beta |=4}|d_\beta |  (if D\equiv 0 set M_4:=0).

Consider the real algebraic hypersurface in \mathbb{R}^n given by  

  F(x)=x^TAx + C(x) + D(x)=0.   (\star )

1.  Prove that there exists \varepsilon >0 --- depending only on \lambda , M_3, M_4 and n --- such that the set of real points of (\star ) does not intersect the punctured ball  

  0 < \|x\|_2 < \varepsilon .

2.  Show that the explicit quantity  

  \varepsilon  := min { \lambda  / (2 n^{3/2} M_3) (if M_3>0),  \sqrt{ \lambda  / (2 n^2 M_4) } (if M_4>0) }  (\dagger )

is admissible, with the convention that a missing entry in the minimum is ignored (equivalently, it is understood to be \infty ).  
In particular, if C\equiv 0 (M_3=0) the first entry in (\dagger ) is dropped, and if D\equiv 0 (M_4=0) the second entry is dropped.  When both C\equiv 0 and D\equiv 0 the hypersurface (\star ) reduces to the empty set {0}, and any \varepsilon >0 works.
kernel_variant.solution:
Write every non-zero x\in \mathbb{R}^n uniquely as x=r \omega  with r=\|x\|_2>0 and \omega \in S^{n-1}:={\omega \in \mathbb{R}^n:\|\omega \|_2=1}.  Because C and D are homogeneous of degrees 3 and 4,

  F(r \omega )=r^2 Q(\omega )+r^3 C(\omega )+r^4 D(\omega ),                         (1)

where Q(\omega )=\omega ^TA\omega , C(\omega )=C(\omega _1,\ldots ,\omega _n) and D(\omega )=D(\omega _1,\ldots ,\omega _n).

Step 1. Angular estimates.  
Positive-definiteness of A gives  

  Q(\omega ) = \omega ^TA\omega  \geq  \lambda .                                          (2)

Put S(\omega ):=\sum _{i=1}^n |\omega _i|.  By Cauchy-Schwarz, S(\omega )\leq \sqrt{n}, whence  

  |C(\omega )| \leq  M_3 S(\omega )^3 \leq  M_3 n^{3/2},                        (3)  
  |D(\omega )| \leq  M_4 S(\omega )^4 \leq  M_4 n^2.                             (4)

Step 2. Radial inequality.  
Combine (1)-(4):

  F(r \omega ) \geq  r^2\lambda  - r^3 n^{3/2}M_3 - r^4 n^2M_4.                (5)

Step 3. Choosing \varepsilon .  
For a given r>0, define  

  g(r):=r^2\lambda  - r^3 n^{3/2}M_3 - r^4 n^2M_4.

The goal is to find \varepsilon  such that g(r)>0 whenever 0<r<\varepsilon .  Note that g(r) is continuous, g(0)=0 and g'(0)=\lambda >0, so a suitable \varepsilon  certainly exists; what remains is to verify that the explicit \varepsilon  in (\dagger ) works.

(a) If M_3>0 impose             r n^{3/2}M_3 < \lambda /2.  
(b) If M_4>0 impose           r^2 n^2 M_4 < \lambda /2.

Whenever a parameter is zero the corresponding inequality is vacuous.  
Because the left sides are increasing in r, the inequalities hold provided  

  r < \lambda /(2 n^{3/2}M_3)  (if M_3>0),  
  r < \sqrt{ \lambda /(2 n^2 M_4) }  (if M_4>0).                        (6)

Define \varepsilon  by (\dagger ); then every 0<r<\varepsilon  satisfies all the relevant conditions (6).  
Substituting in (5) gives

  F(r \omega ) > r^2( \lambda  - \lambda /2 - \lambda /2 ) = 0                     (7)

for all \omega \in S^{n-1} and 0<r<\varepsilon .

Step 4. Exclusion of real zeros.  
If 0<\|x\|_2=r<\varepsilon , writing x=r \omega  yields F(x)=F(r \omega )>0 by (7), so no such x lies on the hypersurface (\star ).  Hence (\star ) has no real points in the punctured ball 0<\|x\|_2<\varepsilon .

Step 5. Degenerate cases.  
* If M_3=0 while M_4>0, the first entry in (\dagger ) is omitted; \varepsilon =\sqrt{\lambda /(2 n^2 M_4)} works by the same argument (inequality (a) is void).  
* If M_4=0 while M_3>0, the second entry is omitted and \varepsilon =\lambda /(2 n^{3/2} M_3) suffices.  
* If M_3=M_4=0, then F(x)=x^TAx>0 for every x\neq 0, so the real zero-set of (\star ) is {0} and any \varepsilon >0 is admissible.  Consistent with the convention, (\dagger ) would read \varepsilon =min{\infty ,\infty }=\infty , leaving the choice of a finite \varepsilon  arbitrary.

Therefore the explicit \varepsilon  prescribed in (\dagger ) --- interpreted with the stated convention --- is always admissible, completing the proof.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: The problem is set in ℝⁿ with n≥3, instead of the original 2-dimensional setting.  
• Additional polynomial degrees: Besides quadratic and cubic parts, a quartic homogeneous part is added, forcing simultaneous control of terms of three different orders.  
• Spectral data: The argument relies on eigen-values of a symmetric matrix; competitors must recall and apply linear-algebraic facts (Rayleigh–Ritz bounds) that were irrelevant in the original problem.  
• Uniform quantitative estimates: One must bound the cubic and quartic pieces uniformly over the entire unit sphere; this demands combinatorial counting of monomials (or a clever use of ℓ¹–ℓ^∞ bounds) rather than the single-angle analysis in two dimensions.  
• Explicit ε: The task is not merely existential; an explicit lower bound in terms of λ, M₃, M₄ and n is requested, adding an optimization step.  
• Interaction of multiple concepts: Competitors must blend homogeneous-polynomial scaling, eigenvalue estimates, and elementary but subtle inequalities to keep track of how three competing orders of r affect the sign of F.  

These layers of complexity collectively raise the problem well above the original’s level: the geometry is higher-dimensional, more algebraic data must be marshalled, and several advanced techniques—spectral estimates, uniform angular bounds, and quantitative inequality solving—are required to reach the conclusion.

Let A be an n \times  n real symmetric positive-definite matrix and denote its smallest

Whenever a parameter is zero the corresponding inequality is vacuous.
